title: Git Foundations
subtitle: Build a clean local history before sharing work anywhere.
description: A beginner Git course with three standard modules and one final capstone. Learners work locally first, moving from an ordinary folder to a readable repository history without introducing branches, remotes, GitHub, or advanced history rewriting.
audience: Absolute beginners who completed Linux Terminal Fundamentals.
Programming learners who want reliable project history before using GitHub.
New developers replacing duplicate final folders with a real version-control workflow.
prerequisites: Complete Linux Terminal Fundamentals or demonstrate equivalent comfort with pwd, ls, cd, mkdir, touch, cat, cp, mv, and rm.
Read and edit simple text files in the provided workspace.
No Python, GitHub account, public network access, or prior version-control experience is required.
goals: Explain the working tree, staging area, commits, and HEAD in practical language.
Initialize a repository and read tracked, untracked, staged, and clean state.
Inspect changes, stage intentionally, and create meaningful commits.
Read history and inspect committed snapshots.
Rename, remove, ignore, and restore files safely.
Complete three cumulative module projects and one integrated local-history capstone.

teachingStyle.tone: story-led, book-like, practical, calm, cumulative, and repository-state-first

preferredTerms.run button: type the command and press Enter
preferredTerms.repository state: the files, staging area, branches, commits, and remotes Git currently records
preferredTerms.check: click Check Answer after verifying the repository state

notes: Use profileId git, fixedLanguage bash, recipeType shell_task, and mode terminal_workspace for every Git code_input.
Use the existing terminal-workspace runner; do not create a Git-specific IDE, runner, recipe type, or programming language.
Grade final repository state with gitExpectations. Use terminalExpectations only when the exact safe command is itself part of the learning goal.
Every terminal instruction must explicitly tell the learner to type the command and press Enter.
Configure the local repository identity as ZoeSkoul Learner and learner@zoeskoul.local before the first commit.
Set the initial branch to main whenever branch naming matters.
Never grade exact commit hashes, machine-dependent timestamps, absolute workspace paths, or decorative git log graph spacing.
Keep independent Try It workspaces isolated with terminalSessionScope exercise; use terminalSessionScope project only for authored progressive projects and capstones.
Use one synopsis sketch for each module project and capstone, followed by exactly the number of cumulative steps declared in projectBrief.stepCountTarget.
The complete solution from project step N becomes the complete starter workspace for step N+1.
Worked examples, Try It tasks, module projects, and final capstones must use different repository stories and different requested outcomes.

Write lessons like a short practical book chapter: begin with the project situation, define the Git idea, show the command shape, trace the repository-state change, warn about one common mistake, and transition naturally into practice.
Do not open lessons with internal curriculum language such as learning contract, milestone count, assessment checklist, or concepts you will demonstrate.
Prepend one standalone reading-only course introduction before the first technical sketch without replacing the first technical lesson.
The final module contains exactly one capstone section and one capstone topic.
Do not require public network access, GitHub credentials, personal access tokens, learner secrets, or an external repository.
Do not introduce branches, merges, remotes, clone, fetch, pull, push, GitHub, stash, reflog, revert, reset, rebase, tags, or force operations in Git Foundations.
Treat main as environment setup, not as a branching lesson.
The final capstone must contain exactly six progressive steps and exactly four authored commits.
